name: Abhinanda Yogin
dates_notes: South-Indian Krama figure of the Mahārtha lineage; dated by his standing as the source-author of the Mahārtha-Mañjarī verses on which Maheśvarānanda's thirteenth-century Parimala commentary builds. Earliest plausible terminus is shortly after Abhinavagupta's Tantrāloka; latest is just before Maheśvarānanda himself.
core_thesis: Non-Vedāntin comparator (Krama / Mahārtha source-verse author). Abhinanda is the figure who composed the seventy-one verses of the *Mahārtha-Mañjarī* — the principal South-Indian Krama text — in the Mahārāṣṭrī Prakrit that gives the work its distinctive register. The position is firmly within the *Krama* tradition: the supreme reality is *Cit* in the form of Kālī, who is the *kāla-saṅkarṣiṇī* — the devourer of time — whose worship through the *krama* (sequence) of twelve Kālīs is the path of recognition (*Mahārtha-Mañjarī* gāthās 1–10, the foundational disposition; gāthās 21–35 on the twelve-Kālī *krama*). The Krama metaphysics is non-dual but specifically *Śākta*: the supreme is articulated through the goddess as the dynamism of consciousness (*MM* gāthās 11–20), and the soteriological focus is on the *krama* by which consciousness sequentially manifests, sustains, withdraws, and re-emerges its objects in each moment of cognition (*MM* gāthās 36–50). The bound subject's task is to recognise this sequenced self-display as the goddess's own self-recognition, by attending to the rhythmic structure of awareness itself (*MM* gāthās 51–65). Abhinanda's verses are the source-text on which Maheśvarānanda will later build his great *Parimala* auto-commentary, and so are the foundational document of the South-Indian Krama tradition that survives the disruption of the Kashmirian Krama lineage.

engaged_works.title: Mahartha-Manjari (verses)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored verses; Maheśvarānanda's Parimala commentary is constructed on Abhinanda's verses as its mūla-text, and the verse-text's authorship is consistently ascribed to Abhinanda within the Krama lineage's self-record.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal acceptance within the South-Indian Krama lineage as the source-text on which Maheśvarānanda's commentary builds.
The Parimala consistently treats the verses as Abhinanda's authoritative composition.

engaged_works.summary: The seventy-one Mahārāṣṭrī Prakrit verses (with intermixed Sanskrit) that constitute the *mūla*-text of the *Mahārtha-Mañjarī* — the principal source-text of the South-Indian Krama tradition. The verses unfold the Krama metaphysics in compact poetic form: Kālī as *kāla-saṅkarṣiṇī*, the devourer of time (*MM* gāthās 1–10); the twelve-fold *krama* of Kālīs as the sequential self-display of consciousness in each moment of cognition — manifestation, sustaining, withdrawal, re-emergence, and the recursive levels of recognition that follow (*MM* gāthās 21–35); the integration of *krama* practice with *spanda* and *Pratyabhijñā* doctrine (*MM* gāthās 11–20); the soteriological focus on attentive recognition of the *krama*-rhythm as the goddess's own self-recognition (*MM* gāthās 51–65). The Prakrit register is distinctive — most Trika and Krama literature is in Sanskrit — and gives the verses a folk-pedagogical accessibility that contrasts with the high scholastic register of the *Tantrāloka*. Maheśvarānanda's *Parimala* will, in the thirteenth century, build the school's authoritative auto-commentary on these verses, but the verses themselves stand as Abhinanda's foundational composition.

reception_notes: Abhinanda's Mahārtha-Mañjarī verses are the foundational document of the South-Indian Krama tradition and the source-text of Maheśvarānanda's Parimala. Modern scholarship on the Krama (Sanderson, Padoux, Silburn) treats the South-Indian Mahārtha lineage as the principal surviving Krama tradition after the disruption of the Kashmirian Krama lineage in the medieval period.
